root_cause: False-positive p99 latency page for the `web` service. The service (calm-svc release stable-1.4.2) is healthy, serving /orders and /healthz successfully with sub-millisecond latency under the existing load generator (2 workers, all succeeding). CPU/memory are negligible, no restarts/OOM, and application logs explicitly report normal operation. The compose project is named sreft-false-alarm; there is no actual latency regression or capacity issue.
actions_taken: Investigated containers, logs, resource usage, network reachability, and endpoint latency for web/load/payments. Confirmed /orders and /healthz return 200 with ~0.2–1ms latency even under elevated synthetic load. Made no configuration, restart, or traffic changes because the service was already healthy; treated the page as a false alarm.
postmortem:
A p99 latency alert fired for `web`, but methodical checks (process health, docker stats, request timing, load-generator status) showed no user-visible or infrastructure problem. The alert was a false positive—likely mis-tuned thresholds, a probe against a non-representative path/target, or a synthetic scenario designed to exercise on-call judgment.

What went well: the service self-reported health clearly; load traffic was already succeeding; investigation stayed non-destructive.

Improvements: (1) validate latency SLOs against measured baseline (sub-ms) so alerts only fire on real regressions; (2) ensure monitors hit the same critical path as production traffic (/orders) and report probe target/path in the page; (3) include recent success-rate and p99 samples in the alert body to speed false-alarm triage; (4) keep “nothing is wrong” style signals only as secondary confirmation, not a substitute for good alert hygiene.
